title: Anthropic Unveils Claude Mythos in Gated Cybersecurity Initiative
summary: Anthropic previewed its most powerful model yet, Claude Mythos, but restricted access to 12 partner organizations due to its dangerous vulnerability-discovery capabilities.
content: Anthropic on April 7 released a limited preview of Claude Mythos, its most capable frontier model, deployed exclusively through a new security initiative called Project Glasswing. Rather than a public launch, access is restricted to 12 partner organizations including Amazon, Apple, Microsoft, Cisco, CrowdStrike, and Palo Alto Networks, who will use it for defensive cybersecurity work. In testing, Mythos identified thousands of zero-day vulnerabilities — many of them one to two decades old — raising alarms about the model's potential for misuse if publicly deployed. Anthropic said the model's capabilities in agentic coding and reasoning were so advanced that it could enable non-experts to turn vulnerabilities into working exploits without human intervention. The company plans to eventually release 'Mythos-class models' publicly, but only once proper safeguards are developed.

title: Meta Launches Muse Spark, Its First Model from the Redesigned AI Program
summary: Meta Superintelligence Labs launched Muse Spark, a multimodal AI model now powering the Meta AI app, with a rollout planned across WhatsApp, Instagram, Facebook, and Meta's smart glasses.
content: Meta Superintelligence Labs released Muse Spark on April 8 — the first model from Zuckerberg's multibillion-dollar AI overhaul, following the disappointing Llama 4 launch in 2025. The model is purpose-built for Meta's product ecosystem, immediately powering the Meta AI app and website in the US, with WhatsApp, Instagram, Facebook, and Messenger integration rolling out in the coming weeks. Muse Spark supports multimodal input combining text and images, runs multiple AI sub-agents simultaneously, and offers two modes: a fast 'Instant' mode and a deliberate 'Thinking' mode for complex reasoning. Meta highlighted the model's health capabilities — particularly its ability to analyze medical charts and images — positioning it as a competitor to ChatGPT Health and Anthropic's Claude for Healthcare. Larger models in the Muse series are in development, and the company said future versions are expected to be open-sourced.

title: Google Releases Gemma 4: Open Models That Outcompete Rivals 20x Their Size
summary: Google DeepMind launched the Gemma 4 family of open models under an Apache 2.0 license, with the flagship 31B model ranking third among all open models globally on the Arena AI leaderboard.
content: Google DeepMind released Gemma 4 on April 2, a family of four open models — E2B, E4B, 26B MoE, and 31B Dense — built on the same research as Gemini 3 and freely available under a commercially permissive Apache 2.0 license. The flagship 31B model currently ranks #3 among open text models worldwide, and the 26B MoE model ranks #6, with both outperforming competing models up to 20 times their size on the Arena AI benchmark. All variants support multimodal inputs including text, images, and audio, with context windows up to 256K tokens, native agentic workflow support via function calling and structured JSON output, and compatibility with 140+ languages. Edge-optimized E2B and E4B models are designed to run entirely offline on mobile devices, Raspberry Pi, and embedded hardware, making frontier-class AI practical without cloud dependency. Since the first Gemma generation, developers have downloaded the family more than 400 million times and created over 100,000 model variants in the community ecosystem.

title: Trump Administration Releases National AI Policy Framework, Seeks State Law Preemption
summary: The White House released a seven-pillar National AI Policy Framework calling on Congress to establish a uniform federal AI standard that would preempt the existing patchwork of conflicting state laws.
content: The Trump administration unveiled its National Policy Framework for Artificial Intelligence on March 20, 2026, building on the December 2025 Executive Order that directed development of legislative recommendations for Congress. The framework spans seven pillars — child protection, AI infrastructure, intellectual property, free speech, innovation, workforce development, and federal preemption — with the overarching goal of replacing the current fragmented landscape of state AI laws with a single national standard. A central recommendation calls on Congress to preempt state AI laws that 'impose undue burdens,' while preserving state authority over traditional police powers, zoning for AI infrastructure, and government procurement decisions. The framework prohibits creating any new federal AI regulatory body and establishes regulatory sandboxes for AI applications, while the already-operational AI Litigation Task Force is tasked with challenging state laws viewed as inconsistent with federal policy. Despite the administration's push for uniformity, the framework already faces significant headwinds, with dozens of states actively advancing their own AI regulatory bills.

title: KPMG: 54% of Enterprises Now Deploying AI Agents as Annual Spending Nears $207M
summary: KPMG's Q1 2026 AI Pulse Survey finds that AI agent deployment has crossed a tipping point, with over half of major organizations running agents in production as spending nearly doubles year-over-year.
content: KPMG's Q1 2026 AI Pulse Survey of 237 U.S.-based C-suite leaders at companies with $1B+ in revenue finds that 54% of organizations are actively deploying AI agents — up from just 12% in early 2024 — marking a definitive shift from experimentation to enterprise-wide production. U.S. organizations project average AI spending of $207 million over the next 12 months, nearly double figures from the same period last year and above the global average of $186 million across 20 countries. Despite the deployment surge, scaling remains the primary challenge: 65% of leaders cite difficulty scaling use cases as their top barrier to demonstrating ROI, up sharply from 33% the prior quarter, while 62% cite workforce skills gaps. Operations (79%) and technology (78%) departments lead in agentic deployment, with 73% of organizations using agents to automate workflows that span multiple functions. A human-led model is emerging as dominant: 57% of leaders now expect people to manage and direct AI agents rather than cede full autonomy, with 87% prioritizing workforce upskilling as their number one focus.

title: Oracle Launches 12 Agentic Applications for Enterprise Finance and Supply Chain
summary: Oracle introduced Fusion Agentic Applications, a suite of 12 enterprise AI apps that autonomously execute finance and supply chain decisions within established business guardrails.
content: Oracle announced Fusion Agentic Applications at its AI World Tour event in New York on April 9, introducing 12 purpose-built agentic apps spanning Oracle Fusion Cloud ERP and SCM platforms. Unlike traditional AI assistants, these applications are powered by coordinated teams of specialized AI agents that proactively reason, decide, and execute tasks autonomously — from settling claims and managing collections to optimizing warehouse operations and guiding shift handoffs — all within existing enterprise security frameworks and approval hierarchies. The 12 apps include a Claims Settlement Workspace, Collectors Workspace, Logistics Execution Command Center, Sourcing Command Center, and Sales Order Command Center, among others, designed to move finance and supply chain teams from passive productivity to AI-driven, proactive execution. Running on Oracle Cloud Infrastructure and powered by leading LLMs, the applications access unified enterprise data, workflows, and transactional context to surface high-impact decisions while automating routine work. Organizations can further extend these capabilities through Oracle AI Agent Studio's new Agentic Applications Builder, which enables custom agent creation without traditional software development.
